title: 第 10 節
en: What we call the Ya(/ur-veda-sa')7ihita is a collection of verses and sacrificial formulas, intended for the use of the priests who, while performing the sacrifice, had to mutter these verses and formulas. Ya(/us^ is the name for these sacrificial formulas, as ya</;7a is the name of sacrifice.
What then is the difference between the collection of hymns of the Rig-veda and the two collections of hymns of the Ya^ur-veda and Sama-veda ?

The collection of hymns of the Rig-veda represents an historical event, like the final collection of the books of the Old Testament. It arose from a desire to preserve from destruction the sacred poetry that was the property of certain families, in order to hand it down as a whole from generation to generation.
The Kha.nda.s or Mantra Period.

I have formerly called the period during which the hymns collected in the Rig-veda were originally composed, the ir/(fandas period, A/^andas being one of ^ This idea of Prof. Weber's has been sufficiently refuted by Burnell, Arsheya-brahma>ia, p. xvi, and by Aufrecht, Rig-veda, second edition, p. xxxvii.

^ The distinction of rik, saman, and yugns is clearly laid down in the Aitareya-aranyaka, II. 3, 6, 8 : 'A rik verse, a gatha, a kumbya (a moral saw) are measured (metrical). A Ya^/us line, an invocation (nigada), and general remarks, these are not measured. A Saman, or any portion of it (geshna, i. e. parvan) is musical.'

SURVEY OF VEDIC LITERATURE. 69 the oldest names for these sacred verses, and I have tried to distinguish it from the period in which these verses were collected and studied as a whole, which I called the Mantra period, mantra being the technical name for these hymns. But later researches have convinced me that with reg^ard to the Rio-'-veda the Mantra period simply represents the closing of the A7^andas period, while with legard to the Ya^urveda and Sama-veda it has now become clear that there never was a Mantra period at all, but that even the first collection of these hymns and formulas belongs to a later period^ that of the prose Brahma?zas, and certainly did not precede that period.
The Prose Brahmawas.

I mentioned before that, according to Hindu authorities, every Veda consists of a collection of hymns, Sa'?7ihitas, and Brahma?! as. These Brahma^^as are the earliest specimens of prose literature in India which we possess, and their object was to describe the elaborate system of sacrifices which had grown up among the Brahmans, and to show how the hymns or portions of the hymns should be used at each sacrifice.

For the performance of these sacrifices, particularly of the great sacrifices, three distinct classes of priests were required. One class had to perform the manual labour, which was very considerable, the clearing of the sacrificial ground, the erection of altars, the lighting of the fire, the preparation of the offerings, &c. They were called Adhvaryus, the labouring priests, and their duties, mixed up with endless speculations, were described in the Brahmav/as of the 70 LECTUEE IV.
Adhvaryus. They formed the Brahma^ias of the Yar/ur-veda.

Another class of priests had to ?ing. They were called Udgatr^s, the singing priests, and their respective duties were in the same way described in the Brahma7ias of the Udgat7^/s, or, as they are also called, the A'Aandogas, i.e. the singers of the A:Aandas. ^ These formed the Brahma?ias of the Sama-veda.

A third class of priests had to recite certain hymns with the utmost correctness of articulation. They were called Hotr^s, the reciting priests, and their duties were described in the Brahma ?< as of the Hotri priests. They formed the Erahma'Jias of the Rig-veda.
The Brahmanas of the Yagur-veda.
We can best study the historical growth of the Brahma?ias in the case of the Adhvaryu priests, the actual performers of the sacrifices.
We possess for the Adhvaryus four ancient works containing explanations of the sacrifice, —

(1) The Kkthaka, belonging to the school of the KafAas, (2) The Kapish^Aala-kafAa Sa'?7ihita, belonging to the school of the Kapish^Aala-ka^^as, (3) The MaitrayaTii Sa^iJiita, belonging to the school of the Maitraya^ias, and (4) The Taittiriyaka.

In these four works the verses to be used by the Adhvaryu priest are given in proper order for each sacrifice, and they are accompanied by prose portions, containing instructions and general observations.

It will be observed that two of them are called Samhitas, though they would more correctly have SURVEY OF VEDIC LITERATURE. 71 been called Brahma-nas. There is. in fact, no other Brahmavm for the Kapish^/^ala-ka^/ms and the Maitrayarayas, besides what is here called their Sa7?diita. The Taittiriyaka, however, exists in two portions, one called Sa77diita, the other Brahma?ia. But here again there is really no distinction between the two, the Brahma'Jia being simply a continuation and appendix of the Sa?7ihita. S a^)/ h ita, in fact, is a misnomer, as applied to the Maitrayaraya and the Kapish^//ala-kaMa SaiJihitas, and, in spite of native tradition, it would be far better to call these collections of the Taittiriyas, Maitrayar<as, and Kapish^/mla-ka^/^as, Brahma?ms.

After a time, however, it was felt to be useful for the priests, when performing the sacrifice, to have a separate collection of the hymns and sacrificial formulas, and another containing the rules of the sacrifice and the explanatory notes. And thus we find in the school of the Var/asaneyins a Sa7nhita, containing nothing but the hymns, and a Brahmana, containing nothing but the explanations. In this form the Ya^ur-veda is called the Bright Yac^'urveda, in contradistinction from the Dark Yap^urveda, in which hymns and explanations are mixed. The Brahma^^a of the Bright Ya^/ur-veda is called the /Satapatha-brahma7ia, and it exists in two texts, as handed down by the two schools of the Madhyandinas and Ka7ivas.

We are thus enabled to see how the so-called Sa77ihita of the Yaf/ur-veda, the collection of verses and formulas to be used by the Adhvaryu priest, arose. It existed first as part and parcel of a Brahmaria, and was afterwards extracted and separated from it for the benefit of the officiating priest. It is therefore 72 LECTURE IV.

really subsequent, not antecedent to the Brahma?ia. It is no more than a manual fpr the use of the Adhvaryus, the labouring priests, extracted from a previous work in prose, which gave a full account of that portion of the sacrifice which this one cl^s of priests, the Adhvaryus, had to perform, together with the necessary verses.
The Brahmawas of the Sama-veda.

Exactly the same seems to have taken place with the Sama-veda. Here too w^e have Brahma^ias, such as the Ta7ic?ya-brahma7ia in twenty-five books, discoursing on that portion of the sacrifice which fell to the share of the singing priests. After a time a hymn-book was felt to be useful, and a Sama-vedasamhita was put together which we still possess in two forms, either as simple texts (Sama-veda-ar/dka), or as adapted to the melodies (Gramageyagana, Araiiyagana)!.

We shall now be better able to see the difference between the collection of the hymns of the Rig-veda, the Rig-veda- sa77ihita, and the other collections of hymns, the Ya^ur-veda-sa'jnhita, and the Sama-vedasa77ihita. The latter were collected for the special benefit of certain classes of priests, and were, so far as we can judge, put together subsequently to the composition of the prose Brahma^ias. They were mere extracts from more ancient Brahma^^as. The Rig-veda-sar/ihita, on the contrar^^, has nothing to do with the sacrifice. It is true that a third class of priests, the Hot?' is, have likewise to recite many SURVEY OF VEDIC LITERATURE. 73 of the hymns of the Rig-veda during the performance of the sacrifice. But there is no collection giving these hymns In the order in which they have to be recited by the Hotri priests. Such a collection would have been analogous to the hymn-books of the labouring and the singing priests, while the collection of the Rig-veda hymns, as Ave possess it, is really an historical collection, carried out in common, as we saw, by a number of Brahmanic families, and by itself utterly useless for sacrificial purposes.
The Brahmana of the Rig'-veda.

It seems that the Hot?^^ priests, the reciters, were the most highly educated Brahmans. It was their duty not only to know the whole of the hymns of the Rig-veda by heart, and to learn to pronounce them with the greatest accuracy, but Hkewise to learn from their Brahma?? as at what part of the sacrifice certain hymns and portions of hymns had to be recited. We still possess two of these Brahma7ias, intended for the use of the reciting priests, (1) The Aitareya-brahma'Jia, belonging, according to Satyavrata, to the >Sakha of the >Sakalas, (2) The Kaushitaki - brahmana, also called the >Sankhayana-brahma?ia.

If, according to the indications contained in these Brahma 77 as, the hymns and verses to be recited by the Hotri priests had been collected and arranged according to the order of the different sacrifices, we should then have had a Rig-veda- sa/'/diita on a level with the Sa9?thitas of the other Vedas. As it is, the Rig-veda-samhita stands by itself. It had a different, not a purely priestly origin, and, so far as we can 74 LECTURE IV.
judge at present, it was anterior, not posterior, to the Brahma^^a period.
The true Veda.
zh: 我們所稱的《梨俱吠陀本集》（Ya(/ur-veda-sa')ihita）是一部詩句與祭祀公式的彙編，供執行祭祀的祭司使用。這些祭司在進行祭祀時，必須口誦這些詩句與公式。Ya(/us^ 是這些祭祀公式的名稱，如同 ya</;7a 是祭祀的名稱一樣。
那麼《梨俱吠陀》的讚歌集與《夜柔吠陀》(Yajur-veda)和《娑摩吠陀》(Sama-veda)的兩部讚歌集之間的區別何在？
梨俱吠陀的讚頌詩集代表了一樁歷史事件，就如同舊約聖經典籍的最終彙編一樣。它源於一種欲望，即保護神聖詩歌免遭毀滅，這些詩歌乃是某些家族的文化遺產，以期能將其作為一個整體世代相傳地流傳下去。
韻頌集（Kha.nda.s）或誦經（Mantra）時期
我曾經將《梨俱吠陀》中所收集的讚歌最初創作的時期稱為「阿蘭達斯時期」(Arandas period)，阿蘭達斯(Arandas)乃是其中之一。* 韋伯教授的這一觀點已被伯內爾(Burnell)在《阿舍亞梵書》(Arsheya-brahmana)第十六頁及奧夫萊希特(Aufrecht)在《梨俱吠陀》第二版第三十七頁充分駁斥。 --- *譯注：原文此處似有脫字，故依文意推測。
^ 在《愛達羅耶阿蘭耶卡》(Aitareya-aranyaka) 第二卷第三、六、八節中，對於梨克詩、薩曼詩和雅朱詩的區分有明確的記載：「梨克詩句、蓋陀詩、庫姆亞詩（道德的格言）是有韻律的。雅朱詩行、祭祀咒文（nigada）和通常的言辭，這些是無韻律的。薩曼詩或其任何部分（geshna，即帕爾瓦）是有音樂性的。」
這些聖詩最古老的名稱，我曾試圖將其與這些詩句作為整體被蒐集和研究的時期相區別，我稱之為誦文時期（Mantra period），誦文（mantra）乃是這些讚歌的技術用語。然而後來的研究已使我確信，就梨俱吠陀而言，誦文時期不過是奧義書時期的結束，而就夜柔吠陀與娑摩吠陀而言，現已明白根本不存在誦文時期，而是連這些讚歌與公式的首次蒐集都屬於一個更晚的時期——即散文梵書時期，當然也不會早於該時期。
散文婆羅門經（Prose Brāhmaṇas）
我之前曾提及，根據印度教當局的記載，每部吠陀都由讚歌集《三昧多》(Saṃhitās) 和《梵書》(Brāhmanas) 組成。這些《梵書》是我們所擁有最早的印度散文文學樣本，其目的是用來描述在婆羅門之中發展成熟的複雜祭祀體系，並闡示如何在每場祭祀中運用讚歌或讚歌的片段。
為了進行這些祭祀，特別是偉大的祭祀，需要三個不同的祭司階級。其中一個階級必須執行手工勞動，工作量非常龐大，包括清理祭祀場地、搭建祭壇、點燃聖火、準備祭品等等。他們被稱為阿德瓦盧斯（Adhvaryus），即勞動祭司，他們的職責與無盡的思辨混雜在一起，被記錄在《梵書》（Brāhmavaṇas）中。 第四講 70
阿特瓦里烏斯（Adhvaryus）。他們構成了夜柔吠陀（Yajur-veda）的婆羅門祭司。
另一類祭司必須進行吟唱。他們被稱為烏德迦塔（Udgatr̥s），即吟唱祭司，其各自的職責同樣在烏德迦塔（Udgātṛ）的梵書中有所記載，或如其也被稱呼的，阿闆多迦（Āḷandogas），即阿闆達（Āḷandas）的吟唱者。¹ 這些構成了薩摩吠陀（Sama-veda）的梵書。
第三類祭司必須以最精確的發音來誦讀某些讚美詩。他們被稱為荷特利（Hotr^s），即誦讀祭司，其職責在《婆羅賀摩經》（Brahma-śāstra）中被描述為荷特利祭司的職責。他們構成了《梨俱吠陀》的婆羅賀摩者（Erahma'Jias）。
雅儒吠陀的梵書（The Brahmanas of the Yagur-veda）。
我們可以藉由研究阿迪瓦尤（Adhvaryu）祭司的例子，最好地研究婆羅門文獻（Brāhmaṇas）的歷史成長，他們乃是犧牲祭祀的實際執行者。
我們為阿闥婆由（Adhvaryus）保有四部古代著作，其中包含了對祭祀的解說，—
（1）《黑色雅祖吠陀》（Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda），屬於迦^Aas學派，（2）《卡皮什^Aala-迦^Aas本集》（Kapiśthāla-kañchas Saṃhitā），屬於卡皮什^Aala-迦^Aas學派，（3）《邁特雷亞^Aias本集》（Maitrayaṇī Saṃhitā），屬於邁特雷亞^Aias學派，以及（4）《泰提利亞書》（Taittirīya）。
在這四部著作中，祭祀祭司（Adhvaryu）所應誦唱的經句按照每一場祭祀的適當順序排列，並伴隨著包含指示和籠統觀察的散文部分。

應當留意到，其中有兩部被稱為《森希塔》(Samhitas)，雖然更為恰當的稱呼應為《梵書》(Brahma-nas)。事實上，除了在此所稱之《森希塔》外，《卡皮沙瑪拉卡森希塔》(Kapishthala-katha)與《邁特雷耶羅耶》(Maitrayaraya)並無其他《梵書》。然而《泰提利耶卡》(Taittiriyaka)則存在於兩個部分，其一稱為《森希塔》，其二稱為《梵書》。但即使在此，兩者間實無本質區別，《梵書》實為《森希塔》之延續與附錄。《森希塔》(Samhita)一詞應用於《邁特雷耶羅耶森希塔》與《卡皮沙瑪拉卡森希塔》實屬用詞不當，儘管土著傳統如此，將泰提利耶人、邁特雷耶羅耶人與卡皮沙瑪拉卡人之這些文獻匯編，改稱為《梵書》實為更加妥當。

然而，經過一段時間後，祭司們在進行祭祀時，若能擁有一部專門蒐集讚歌與祭祀公式的文獻，以及另一部包含祭祀規則與解釋性註疏的文獻，便被認為頗有用處。因此，在跋羅闍尼耶（Var/asaneyins）學派中，我們發現了一部《本集》（Sa7nhita）僅包含讚歌，以及一部《梵書》（Brahmana）僅包含解釋。以此形式，《夜柔吠陀》（Ya^ur-veda）被稱為光明《夜柔吠陀》（Bright Yac^'urveda），以與黑暗《夜柔吠陀》（Dark Yap^urveda）相區別，後者則混合了讚歌與解釋。光明《夜柔吠陀》的《梵書》被稱為《百路梵書》（/Satapatha-brahma7ia），現存於兩個版本，分別由中日派（Madhyandinas）與卡拉瓦派（Ka7ivas）兩個學派代代相傳。
我們因此得以看出所謂的《夜柔吠陀》（Yajur-veda）《本集》（Sanhitā）如何產生——這是供奉行祭祀的阿特瓦里雍祭司（Adhvaryu）使用的詩句與咒式的彙編。它最初作為《梵書》（Brāhmaṇa）的組成部分而存在，之後才被提取並從中分離出來，以便祭祀的執行者使用。因此 72 第四講
實際上是後於《梵書》（Brāhmaṇa）而非先於其之作。它不過是為從事勞動祭祀的祭司階級雅德瓦盧斯（Adhvaryus）所編製的手冊，摘錄自一部先前的散文著作，該著作曾詳細說明祭祀儀式的某個部分——即這一類祭司雅德瓦盧斯必須執行的部分，並附以必要的讚頌詩句。
娑摩吠陀的梵書。

完全相同的情況似乎也發生在娑摩吠陀（Sama-veda）上。這裡同樣有梵書（Brahmaṇa），如二十五卷的塔底耶梵書（Taittirīya-brahmaṇa），論述了唱誦祭司所分擔的那部分祭祀。經過一段時間後，人們感到一部讚美詩集是有用的，因此編纂了《娑摩吠陀‧本集》（Sama-veda-saṃhita），我們至今仍保有其兩種形式：一為單純的文本形式（Sama-veda-aṛdhka），或一為適應於旋律的版本（Gramageyagana, Araiiyagana）。

我們現在應能更好地看出《梨俱吠陀》(Rig-veda-saṃhita) 的讚歌集與其他讚歌集——《夜柔吠陀》(Yajur-veda-saṃhita) 與《娑摩吠陀》(Sama-veda-saṃhita)——之間的差異。後者是為某些祭司階級的特殊利益而蒐集的，據我們所能判斷，是在散文《梵書》(Brāhmanas) 的成書之後才編纂的。它們只是更古老之《梵書》的摘錄。相反地，《梨俱吠陀經集》與祭禮毫無關係。誠然，第三類祭司——何多利祭司 (Hotṛi) ——在執行祭禮的過程中，也必須吟誦許多《梨俱吠陀》的讚歌。但卻沒有任何一部集子按照何多利祭司所必須吟誦的順序來編排這些讚歌。這樣的集子本應與勞動祭司和歌唱祭司的讚歌集相類似；而我們所擁有的《梨俱吠陀》讚歌集，實則是一部歷史性的集編，乃由許多婆羅門家族共同進行編纂的，如我們前所見，其本身對祭禮目的毫無用處。
梨俱吠陀的梵書

似乎霍特里（Hotṛ）祭司，亦即誦經者，乃是受教育程度最高的婆羅門。他們的職責不僅要將梨俱吠陀的全部讚歌銘記於心，並學習以最高的準確性誦讀這些讚歌，同樣重要的是，他們還要從其梵書（Brāhmaṇa）中學習在祭祀的哪個部分應當誦讀某些讚歌及讚歌的片段。我們仍保有其中兩部梵書，乃是為誦經祭司的使用而編寫的：（1）《愛達瑞亞梵書》（Aitareya-brāhmaṇa），按照薩蒂亞瓦拉塔的說法，屬於沙卡羅（Śākalā）學派；（2）《考希塔基梵書》（Kaushitaki-brāhmaṇa），亦稱為《桑卡亞那梵書》（Śāṅkhāyana-brāhmaṇa）。

根據梵書（Brahma 77 as）所包含的指示，若熱祭司（Hotri）所誦唸的讚歌與詩句曾被蒐集並按照不同祭祀的次序編排，那麼我們本應獲得一部與其他吠陀的本集（Sa9?thitas）相當的梨俱吠陀本集（Rig-veda-sa/'/diita）。然而實際情況並非如此，梨俱吠陀本集（Rig-veda-samhita）獨立存在。它具有不同的、而非純粹祭司的起源，就我們所能了解的範圍而言 74 第四講。
現在的判斷來看，它是先於婆羅門時期（Brahma^^a period），而非後於該時期。
真正的吠陀
